metadata.name: Sovereign Design Patterns Intelligence Dataset
metadata.description: Software design patterns with implementations, anti-patterns, use cases, and refactoring guidance for code intelligence routing

samples.intent: Ensure a class has only one instance and provide a global point of access
samples.anti_pattern: Creating multiple instances of shared resources like database pools or config objects, leading to inconsistent state
trade_offs: Global state can make testing difficult
Thread safety concerns in concurrent environments

samples.intent: Define a one-to-many dependency so when one object changes state, all dependents are notified
samples.anti_pattern: Tight coupling between components where changes in one module require modifications in many others
trade_offs: Memory leaks if listeners not properly removed

samples.intent: Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable
samples.anti_pattern: Large if/elif chains or switch statements that select behavior based on type or condition flags
trade_offs: Client must be aware of available strategies

samples.intent: Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation
samples.anti_pattern: Constructors with many parameters (telescoping constructor) making object creation error-prone
trade_offs: Builder must be updated when new fields added

samples.intent: Prevent cascading failures by stopping requests to a failing service after a threshold
samples.anti_pattern: Unbounded retry loops that overwhelm failing services and cause cascading outages
trade_offs: May reject valid requests during recovery

samples.intent: Mediate between domain logic and data mapping layers using a collection-like interface

implementation.code:
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from typing import Generic, TypeVar, Optional
from dataclasses import dataclass

T = TypeVar('T')

class Repository(ABC, Generic[T]):
    @abstractmethod
    async def find_by_id(self, id: str) -> Optional[T]:
        ...

    @abstractmethod
    async def find_all(self, limit: int = 100, offset: int = 0) -> list[T]:
        ...

    @abstractmethod
    async def save(self, entity: T) -> T:
        ...

    @abstractmethod
    async def delete(self, id: str) -> bool:
        ...

@dataclass
class User:
    id: str
    name: str
    email: str

class UserRepository(Repository[User]):
    def __init__(self, db):
        self._db = db

    async def find_by_id(self, id: str) -> Optional[User]:
        row = await self._db.fetch_one('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', id)
        return User(**row) if row else None

    async def find_all(self, limit: int = 100, offset: int = 0) -> list[User]:
        rows = await self._db.fetch_all('SELECT * FROM users LIMIT $1 OFFSET $2', limit, offset)
        return [User(**row) for row in rows]

    async def save(self, entity: User) -> User:
        await self._db.execute(
            'INSERT INTO users (id, name, email) VALUES ($1, $2, $3) ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE SET name=$2, email=$3',
            entity.id, entity.name, entity.email
        )
        return entity

    async def delete(self, id: str) -> bool:
        result = await self._db.execute('DELETE FROM users WHERE id = $1', id)
        return result.rowcount > 0

samples.anti_pattern: Scattering SQL queries throughout business logic, making database changes require touching many files
trade_offs: May not fit document/graph databases well

samples.intent: Encapsulate a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, and undo operations
samples.anti_pattern: Direct method calls that cannot be undone, replayed, or scheduled for later execution

samples.intent: Chain processing steps where each can transform input, short-circuit, or pass to next handler
samples.anti_pattern: Deeply nested callback chains or sequential if-checks that are difficult to reorder or extend

samples.intent: Allow an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes
samples.anti_pattern: Boolean flags and nested conditionals tracking object lifecycle state, leading to invalid state combinations
trade_offs: Adding states requires updating all transitions
May be overkill for simple 2-3 state objects

samples.intent: Store state changes as a sequence of immutable events rather than mutable current state
samples.anti_pattern: Directly mutating database records with no history, losing the ability to audit, debug, or reconstruct past states
